questionText: Consider the statement: 'if a quadrilateral has four equal sides, then it is a square'. Show that this statement is false. Then state a corrected biconditional characterisation of a square of the form 'a quadrilateral is a square $\Leftrightarrow$ it has four equal sides AND at least one right angle', prove both directions, and explain why the pair of conditions {four equal sides, one right angle} is necessary and sufficient for a square.

questionDiagram.description: Two quadrilaterals drawn side by side, both with four equal-length sides, to contrast a rhombus (no right angle) against a square (right angles), showing that equal sides alone do not force a square.

elements.label: Rhombus
elements.note: four equal sides but not a square (counterexample)

elements.label: Square
elements.note: four equal sides and right angles

steps.workingLatex: P:\ \text{quadrilateral has four equal sides}, \quad S:\ \text{it is a square}; \quad \text{claim } P \Rightarrow S
steps.explanation: The statement is a one-way implication, so it can be broken by a single shape satisfying $P$ but not $S$. Naming the conditions makes the target of the counterexample precise.

steps.workingLatex: \text{square: four equal sides AND four right angles}; \quad \text{four equal sides alone: rhombus}
steps.explanation: A square demands both equal sides and right angles, whereas 'four equal sides' by itself only defines a rhombus. Pinning down definitions is what lets us spot the gap in the claim.

steps.workingLatex: \text{find a quadrilateral with four equal sides that is not a square}
steps.explanation: Disproving $P \Rightarrow S$ requires exactly one shape with equal sides but the wrong angles. A rhombus that is not a square is the natural candidate.
This rhombus has all four sides equal yet none of its angles is a right angle, so it cannot be a square. It satisfies $P$ but violates $S$, exactly what a counterexample needs.

steps.description: Conclude the original statement is false.
steps.workingLatex: P \text{ true}, \ S \text{ false for this shape} \;\Rightarrow\; P \Rightarrow S \text{ is FALSE}
steps.explanation: One valid counterexample is enough to refute the universal claim. Having four equal sides is therefore not sufficient to force a square.

steps.description: Note that equal sides is still necessary.
steps.workingLatex: S \Rightarrow P \ (\text{a square does have four equal sides})
steps.explanation: Although equal sides do not guarantee a square, every square does have equal sides, so the condition is necessary. It is the sufficiency, not the necessity, that fails.

steps.workingLatex: \text{rhombus fails only because its angles are not } 90^{\circ}
steps.explanation: The single feature separating our counterexample from a square is the angle. Adding a right-angle requirement should close the gap, motivating the corrected statement.

steps.workingLatex: S \Leftrightarrow C, \quad C:\ \text{four equal sides AND at least one right angle}
steps.explanation: We propose that a square is characterised by equal sides together with one right angle. Writing it as a biconditional signals that both implications must now be proved.

steps.description: Prove the easy direction S ⇒ C.
steps.workingLatex: \text{square} \Rightarrow \text{four equal sides and four right angles} \Rightarrow C
steps.explanation: A square by definition has four equal sides and four right angles, so it certainly has at least one right angle. This direction is immediate from the definition.

steps.description: Begin the reverse direction C ⇒ S.
steps.workingLatex: \text{assume four equal sides and one } 90^{\circ} \text{ angle} \Rightarrow \text{shape is a rhombus (a parallelogram)}
steps.explanation: Four equal sides make the quadrilateral a rhombus, and every rhombus is a parallelogram with opposite sides parallel. Parallelogram angle properties are the key to propagating the single right angle.

steps.workingLatex: \text{consecutive angles of a parallelogram sum to } 180^{\circ}: \ 180^{\circ} - 90^{\circ} = 90^{\circ}
steps.explanation: In a parallelogram each pair of adjacent angles is supplementary. So if one angle is $90^{\circ}$, both of its neighbours must also be $90^{\circ}$.

steps.description: Use equal opposite angles for the fourth.
steps.explanation: Opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal, so the remaining angle matches the first at $90^{\circ}$. That forces every angle to be a right angle.

steps.workingLatex: \text{four equal sides} + \text{four right angles} = \text{square} \;\Rightarrow\; C \Rightarrow S
steps.explanation: A quadrilateral with four equal sides and four right angles is by definition a square. So the pair of conditions is enough to force a square.

steps.workingLatex: S \Rightarrow C \ \text{and}\ C \Rightarrow S \;\Rightarrow\; S \Leftrightarrow C
steps.explanation: Both implications hold, so the corrected characterisation is a genuine two-way equivalence. The definition and the condition describe exactly the same shapes.

steps.workingLatex: \text{equal sides only} \to \text{rhombus}; \quad \text{one right angle only} \to \text{e.g. rectangle}
steps.explanation: A rhombus has equal sides without right angles, and a non-square rectangle has a right angle without equal sides. Each condition alone fails, which is why the square needs both together.

steps.workingLatex: \{\text{four equal sides},\ \text{one right angle}\} \text{ is necessary and sufficient for a square}
steps.explanation: Together the two conditions are both required by a square and enough to guarantee one, so as a pair they are necessary and sufficient. The original claim failed only because it dropped the right-angle half.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The statement is FALSE: a rhombus with angles $60^{\circ},120^{\circ},60^{\circ},120^{\circ}$ has four equal sides but is not a square. Corrected: a quadrilateral is a square $\Leftrightarrow$ it has four equal sides AND at least one right angle. Reverse direction: four equal sides make it a rhombus (a parallelogram), so one $90^{\circ}$ angle forces all four angles to be $90^{\circ}$, giving a square. Hence the pair {four equal sides, one right angle} is necessary and sufficient for a square, while neither condition alone suffices.
